coredata.dc:title: Influence of precursor choice on the luminescence color purity of carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Manon, Priti
Arya, Anil
Sharma, Suchinder K.
coredata.dc:description:
The quest for new luminescent materials, particularly carbon based, is ongoing. Herein, we present results on the synthesis of water-soluble carbon dots via a hydrothermal process using L-ascorbic acid, sodium carbonate, and urea as chemical precursors. The X-ray diffraction patterns suggest that the samples possess sharp maxima corresponding to (002) reflections and sp
3
disorder. The crystallite size was evaluated using the Scherrer formula, a modified Scherrer formula that considers strain using the Williamson–Hall plot, and a Zeta sizer. The diameter is approximately 65 nm. The zeta potential, or surface charge, in all samples shows negative values ranging from −30 to −60 mV, indicating the suitability of the nanoparticles for biological applications. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy reveals three prominent signatures in our samples at 3300, 1640, and 1390 cm−1, corresponding to O-H, C=O, and C-H vibrations, respectively. The prominent signal in UV-visible absorption spectroscopy is observed in the UV region, with a minor contribution in the visible region, indicating a combined role of the graphitic core and surface states. Photoluminescence emission was obtained in the blue region, and all samples could be excited with near-UV light (around 365 nm). We explored the potential of using blue-emitting nanomaterials for solid-state lighting applications and obtained a color purity of 85% for samples prepared with L-ascorbic acid and urea, suggesting their suitability for lighting application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Photoluminescence
Blue emission
Solid-state lighting
Color purity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photostability, color purity and lighting application

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Suchinder K. Sharma thanks DST-SERB India (SRG/2022/000415) for their financial support. This work was partially carried out using the facilities of Central Instrumentation Facility (CIF), Amity University Punjab, Mohali, India.

S.K.S. thanks DST-SERB India ( SRG/2022/000415 ) for their financial support. This work was partially carried out using the facilities of Central Instrumentation Facility (CIF), Amity University Punjab, Mohali .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD of water soluble carbon dots HCD1, HCD2 & HCD3.
(a) W–H Plot Of water soluble carbon dots HCD1, HCD2 & HCD3.
(a) The variation of crystallite size for the three samples, (b) Zeta potential water soluble samples, HCD1, HCD2 & HCD3.
(a) FTIR reflectance spectra for three different samples, HCD1, HCD2 & HCD3.
UV–Visible absorption spectra of the three samples, HCD1, HCD2 and HCD3.

(a, c, e) PLE and PL emission spectra of samples, HCD1, HCD2 and HCD3; and (b, d, f) Normalized PL emission spectra of the three samples, respectively. In the Figure, X represents excitation wavelength to obtain PL emission, while M represents emission maximum for which the PLE is obtained.
Photostability of the synthesized carbon dots from different precursors.
(a) CIE color coordinate diagram showing color purity of all the three samples upon excitation at 355 ± 10 nm, (b) zoom in of the region-of-interest.
Variation of crystallite size after calculation using Scherrer formula, modified Scherrer formula, and determination of crystallite size using zeta sizer.

head.ce:title: Influence of precursor choice on the luminescence color purity of carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Amity School of Physical Sciences, Amity University Punjab, IT City, Sector–82A, Mohali 140306, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Amity School of Physical Sciences, Amity University Punjab

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Amity School of Physical Sciences, Amity University Punjab, IT City, Sector–82A, Mohali–140306, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The quest for new luminescent materials, particularly carbon based, is ongoing. Herein, we present results on the synthesis of water-soluble carbon dots via a hydrothermal process using L-ascorbic acid, sodium carbonate, and urea as chemical precursors. The X-ray diffraction patterns suggest that the samples possess sharp maxima corresponding to (002) reflections and sp
3
disorder. The crystallite size was evaluated using the Scherrer formula, a modified Scherrer formula that considers strain using the Williamson–Hall plot, and a Zeta sizer. The diameter is approximately 65 nm. The zeta potential, or surface charge, in all samples shows negative values ranging from −30 to −60 mV, indicating the suitability of the nanoparticles for biological applications. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy reveals three prominent signatures in our samples at 3300, 1640, and 1390 cm−1, corresponding to O-H, C=O, and C-H vibrations, respectively. The prominent signal in UV-visible absorption spectroscopy is observed in the UV region, with a minor contribution in the visible region, indicating a combined role of the graphitic core and surface states. Photoluminescence emission was obtained in the blue region, and all samples could be excited with near-UV light (around 365 nm). We explored the potential of using blue-emitting nanomaterials for solid-state lighting applications and obtained a color purity of 85% for samples prepared with L-ascorbic acid and urea, suggesting their suitability for lighting application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:sections.ce:para: Carbon based materials are of immense importance in various applications, including bioimaging [1], anticounterfeiting [2], and optical sensors [3]. They are also utilized in other biological domains, such as biosensors [4], drug delivery [5], and gene transmission [6], due to their biocompatibility and low toxicity [7]. Carbon dots can be based on graphene, carbon, or polymer types [8]. One reason for the significant interest from the scientific community in carbon dots is the possibility of synthesizing them in various shapes (or morphologies), which exhibit different optical properties [9,10]. The carbon dots can be categorized as either carbon nanoparticles or carbon quantum dots [11]. The nanoparticles exhibit an amorphous nature and do not show a quantum confinement effect. In contrast, carbon quantum dots display a strong quantum confinement effect, allowing their luminescence properties to be tuned between the blue–green and red regions of the electromagnetic spectrum, depending on the size of the dots. Additionally, carbon dots possess strong electrochemical properties due to their controlled size, doping, and/or functionalization [12]. Carbon dots can be prepared using several methods, including top-down approaches such as hydrothermal, microwave, electrochemical, solvothermal, and oxidative cleavage methods. Additionally, bottom-up approaches, such as pyrolysis and carbonization using organic molecules, are also employed [13]. One interesting synthesis procedure is the hydrothermal synthesis route. In this method, the precursors are mixed with a solvent and sealed in a hydrothermal bomb, where they are heated at elevated temperatures. The precursors can include organic molecules such as chitosan, glucose, or citric acid [14], as well as natural products used for green synthesis [15]. In the early stages of research, the focus on carbon dots (CDs) was mainly on their fluorescence emission characteristics, such as wavelength, color, and brightness, with less attention given to other fascinating optical phenomena. However, recent developments have significantly improved the external quantum efficiency (EQE) and brightness of electroluminescent (EL) LEDs. Studies indicate that the theoretical maximum EQE for fluorescent CDs is 5%, with the current highest value achieved being 4% [16].

Water soluble carbon dots possess distinctive properties with the wide range of applications due to biocompatibility and low toxicity, strong fluorescence, ease of functionalization and environmental friendliness. The most remarkable feature of carbon dots is their strong and tunable fluorescence depending on their size, surface states that trap as well as releases energy and presence of functional groups (such as carboxylic acids, hydroxyl and amino groups) on its surface they emit light from blue to red range [17]. They also show excitation-dependent fluorescence in which the wavelength of light used to excite them can change their emission color. They do not lose their fluorescence under prolonged exposure to light due to high photostability. The smaller carbon dots often emit light at shorter wavelength, i.e., blue light emission and the larger grains emit in the red region. The functionalization can increase their solubility, stability and interaction with biological molecules [18]. The exact size of carbon dots depends on the type of synthesis method along with its initial conditions during its formation.

Following information from the research literature, we pursue the present work to understand: (a) the influence of different precursors on the luminescence properties of carbon dots, and (b) the determination of color purity in the samples for potential applications in solid-state lighting. In this context, we prepared a series of three samples using three different precursors
v i a
the hydrothermal method using water as a solvent. X-ray diffraction and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy patterns confirm the formation of carbon dots. This formation was further confirmed using UV-visible absorption spectroscopy, which indicates that the samples possess absorption due to a graphitic core and surface defects. By combining photoluminescence excitation and emission results, and CIE coordinates, we determined the color purity of the blue-emitting samples and observed a high purity of ∼ 85% for the samples prepared from L-ascorbic acid and urea.

ce:section.ce:para:
Analytical grade chemicals, L-ascorbic acid (C
6
H
8
O
6
, 99.7%), sodium carbonate (Na
2
CO
3
, 99.9%), and urea (CH
4
N
2
O, 99.5%) were used as precursors for the synthesis. All the chemicals were procured from Sisco Research Laboratories (SRL) Pvt. Ltd., India. In a typical procedure, we prepared three different samples using the hydrothermal method. Stoichiometric amounts of the precursors were dissolved in 12.5 mL of Milli-Q water and stirred using a magnetic stirrer at room temperature for 30 min. The resulting solutions were then transferred to Teflon beakers, placed in an autoclave, and heated at 180 ° C for 8 h. The autoclave was allowed to cool down inside a furnace, and the prepared solutions were filtered for further characterization. The samples were coded HCD1, HCD2, and HCD3 for the synthesis using precursors, L-ascorbic acid, sodium carbonate, and urea, respectively.

The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern were recorded on a Rigaku Miniflex 600 instrument with Cu-target emitting K
α
radiation ( λ = 1.54060 Å), to assess the crystalline and/or amorphous nature of the samples. The measurements were performed at a fixed scan speed of 0.01 ° /s over 2 θ angles between 10 to 80 ° . The surface functional groups and bond formation was determined on a Nicolet™ Summit LITE spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, US) between wavenumbers 400 to 4000 cm−1, and an accuracy of 0.05 cm−1. The particle size and zeta potential of the carbon dots (CDs) were determined using a Malvern Zetasizer Lab Blue, utilizing a 10 × 10 disposable polystyrene cell (DTS0012) and a disposable folded capillary cell (DTS1070) for the measurements. The Photoluminescence excitation (PLE) and emission spectra were recorded using a Fluoromax Plus-C spectrofluorometer from Horiba Scientific. The data is collected using a photon counting PMT R928P. The photostability experiments were conducted upon excitation with Crompton 40 W (4000 lumen) LED Eco bulb. The CIE coordinated were calculated on Osram color calculator.

The crystallographic structure of the three samples was analyzed using X-ray diffraction (XRD) technique. The results are shown in Fig. 1. The XRD pattern exhibits two distinct behaviors: (a) a broad peak between 20 and 30 ° in 2 θ angles; and (b) sharp reflections indicating the crystalline behavior of the as-synthesized samples. The XRD patterns of all three samples, HCD1, HCD2, and HCD3, show broad amorphous peaks at 2 θ values of 23.91, 26.78, and 25.10°, respectively, confirming their graphitic nature with (002) reflections and significant sp
3
disorder [19]. Compared to graphite and graphene, the broader XRD peaks of the three samples can be attributed to small crystallite sizes and/or the amorphous nature of the samples. Samples HCD1 and HCD3 exhibit similar XRD patterns, while sample HCD2 shows additional reflections, with the most prominent reflections (highest intensity) at 16.20° (200) and 16.50° (010). These reflections correspond to carbon in sodium carbonate [20]. The XRD results suggest that all samples possess smaller grain sizes and some crystalline behavior due to carbon atoms. Additionally, XRD can be used to assess the purity of carbon quantum dots [21]. The interlayer spacing values of 0.37, 0.34, and 0.35 nm for HCD1, HCD2, and HCD3, respectively, are larger than that of bulk graphite (0.33 nm), indicating a disordered graphitic core. These findings confirm that the carbon dots have an amorphous nature, attributed to the closely packed C-N, CC, C=C, and CO bonds within the carbon dots. This value also suggests the disorder or amorphous nature of the samples due to oxygen-containing functionalities [22].

We further determined the crystallite size of the samples using two different techniques: (a) by analyzing the XRD results and calculating the size considering the stress (and strain) components using the Williamson–Hall plot [23]; and (b) by measuring the size using a Zeta sizer. The latter will be discussed in the next section.

The apparent crystalline size of all the water-soluble samples was calculated using the Debye–Scherrer formula, [24] using Eq. (1).
(1)
D =
k . λ
β ⋅ C o s θ
where, D represents the crystallite size, k is the dimensionless shape factor (0.94 considered), λ is X-ray wavelength used (1.54060 Å), and β is full width at half maximum (FWHM).

The value of β is corrected by accounting for the broadening due to the Si standard (or instrumental error). The Scherrer equation focuses solely on the effect of crystallite size on XRD peak broadening, disregarding lattice microstructures such as inherent strain caused by flaws in nanocrystals, grain boundaries, and stacking faults. To account for strain-induced broadening, the Williamson–Hall (W–H) method is employed, providing a more accurate approach to determine (and correct) the crystallite size calculated from the Scherrer formula. Both crystallite size and strain contribute to XRD peak broadening, and the W–H method uses tan θ to differentiate between these two effects, in contrast to the Scherrer’s 1/cos θ relationship. By combining the contributions of small crystallite size and microstrain, the W–H method separates the broadening into its size- and strain-related components, as expressed in Eq. (2).
(2)
β
p u r e
=
β
s i z e
+
β
s t r a i n

The contribution from the above two components in Eq. (2) are based on Ref. 25, as given below:
(3)
β
s i z e
=
λ
D ⋅ C o s θ
and
β
s t r a i n
= η ⋅ t a n θ

Putting values from Eq. (3) in Eq. (2), we get Eq. (4) as follows:
(4)
β
p u r e
=
λ
D ⋅ C o s θ
+ η ⋅ t a n θ

Or,
(5)
β
p u r e
⋅ C o s θ
λ
=
1
D
+ η ⋅
S i n θ
λ

Hence, when a graph is plotted between Sin θ and [
β p u r e
⋅ Cos θ ]/ λ , a straight line provides an intercept (y) whose inverse provides a value of size, while the slope provides the microstrain [26]. The results obtained from modified Scherrer equation are shown in Fig. 2, and obtained from values are tabulated in Table 1.

The values for samples HCD1 and HCD2 are found to be consistent with the each other at 60 ± 6 nm. However, the values of crystallite size for sample HCD3 are determined to be much lower at 12 nm after considering the strain component in Scherrer formula.

The determination of surface charge (potential) is also an important parameter for the application of carbon-based nanomaterials in any biological application. The zeta potential measures the electrostatic charge on the surface of the particles. A more negative zeta potential (in value) indicates that the particles have an excess of negative charges due to the presence of functional groups such as carboxylic acid and hydroxyl groups. Negatively charged carbon dots can interact with positively charged biomolecules, like proteins or cell membranes. Additionally, a negative zeta potential helps maintain the stability of carbon dot suspensions by preventing aggregation. Furthermore, negatively charged groups can serve as anchor points for surface modification and functionalization. The results for the crystallite size and zeta potential are shown in Fig. 3(a, b).

From the perspective of crystallite size [Fig. 3(a)], we observe a distribution of crystallite sizes in samples HCD1 and HCD2. The sample HCD1 exhibits crystallites with two different sizes: one group around 17 nm and another around 65 nm. Similarly, sample HCD2 shows crystallites with sizes of 35 nm and 70 nm. In contrast, sample HCD3 possesses a larger grain size and does not show such variation in crystallite size, with a size found to be ∼ 100 nm. The values for HCD1 and HCD2 are in agreement with the sizes obtained from XRD results,
c f
. Table 1. The reason for the large variation in sample HCD3 is unknown at this time. Although, one possible reason for such a change could be better cross-linking of carbon atoms via formation of C-N bond for HCD3 sample. In HCD1 and HCD2, oxygen containing functional group may disrupt the carbon network while HCD3 (urea) has low oxygen content or less defects. The possibility of different decomposition mechanisms of different precursors cannot be ruled out.

From Fig. 3(b), the zeta potentials of the water-soluble carbon dots HCD1, HCD2, and HCD3 are determined to be −31.21 mV, −55.85 mV, and −35.01 mV, respectively. A negative zeta potential indicates that the carbon dots have a negative charge due to the deprotonation of hydroxyl and carboxylic groups on their surface. The addition of oxygen-rich functional groups enhances the negative charge on the surfaces of hydroxyl (-OH), carboxylic (-COOH), and carbonyl groups, as oxygen is highly electronegative and has a strong affinity for electrons, resulting in a greater negative charge. This charge is further amplified when these groups lose a proton to form COO
−
[27]. In contrast, removing oxygen-containing groups decreases the negative charge; with the loss of these electronegative groups, the electron density is reduced, leading to a less negatively charged surface [27].

The particle size of HCD1 and HCD2 is nearly identical, approximately 70 nm (Fig. 3a). However, there is a noticeable difference in their zeta potential values (Fig. 3b). Zeta potential, which reflects the effective surface charge of particles, is commonly used to assess their stability in colloidal systems. A comparison reveals that HCD1 has a lower zeta potential compared to HCD2. The negative zeta potential observed in both samples can be attributed to the dense electron cloud surrounding the carbon dots. HCD2, with its higher zeta potential, exhibits better electrostatic stability, whereas HCD1, with its lower zeta potential, is more prone to coagulation or aggregation over a short period.

The surface charge of carbon dots plays a significant role in studying their behavior in both extracellular and intracellular environments. In the case of intracellular interactions, the surface charge helps determine how carbon dots interact with living cells, organelles, biomolecules, tissues, nucleic acids, and proteins. In contrast, in extracellular contexts, the surface charge affects the stability and dispersion of carbon dots, as well as their capacity to attach to cell membranes or proteins. These interactions are essential for applications in drug delivery and bioimaging [28]. Therefore, controlling surface charge is crucial to enhance the effectiveness of carbon dots in biological systems. This behavior is important in fields such as surface chemistry, catalysis, and molecular interactions, where charge distribution significantly influences reactivity.

The results from the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy of three samples are shown in Fig. 4. The samples HCD1 and HCD3 shows two prominent absorption bands at 3300 and 1640 cm−1, while sample HCD2 exhibit an additional band at 1390 cm−1. In samples HCD1, HCD2, and HCD3, a common vibrational peak ∼ 3300 cm−1 corresponds to O-H or N-H stretching vibrations, which indicates the presence of hydroxyl (-OH) or amine (-NH) groups. Another peak which appears at ∼ 1640 cm−1 is associated with CO stretching vibrations, suggesting the presence of carbonyl groups. This peak may also correspond to C=C stretching in alkenes or aromatic rings as reported earlier [29].
The UV-visible absorption spectra for the three samples is shown in Fig. 5. The complete spectra can be divided into five different regions (marked I to V).

These five regions are attributed to either the graphitic core ( λ
< 300 nm) or graphitic surface states ( λ
> 300 nm). Absorption is notably stronger in the UV regions B and C, with very little or no contribution in the visible region above 400 nm. Sample HCD1 shows prominent absorption at 265 nm, while samples HCD2 and HCD3 exhibit the highest absorption around 230 nm. The 230 nm band corresponds to π
→
π
⋆
transitions assigned to the C=C bonds in the aromatic structure of graphene oxide [31,32]. However, these transitions show a significant red-shift of approximately 35 nm to 265 nm in reduced graphene oxide. This indicates that among our samples, HCD1 shows absorption due to reduced graphene oxide, while HCD2 and HCD3 show absorption due to graphene oxide. All samples also exhibit a small hump (absorption) around 320 nm, which corresponds to n
→
π
⋆
transitions of C O in carbon dots. This small signal, which is above the background level, can be attributed to surface states containing electron lone pairs,
c f
. Fig. 5.

In the literature, it has been reported that as the synthesis temperature increases, the growth of the graphitic core accelerates, leading to a simultaneous decrease in surface states. Such changes are expected to be visible in the absorption spectra with a change in the relative bands. Additionally, some studies report absorption bands around 550 and 595 nm. These bands correspond to different transitions of C=S and S=O, typically due to the presence (or doping) of sulfur from the starting materials (precursors) [33]. In our samples, we observe little absorption in this region compared to the other bands in the UV range. Overall, the variations and modifications in the UV-visible absorption spectra among the different samples can be attributed to changes in composition and electronic configuration within the samples.

The luminescence of carbon dots (CDs) is primarily governed by the degree of graphitization in their structure; however, the exact mechanism responsible for controlling their fluorescence remains unclear [34]. In this context, we measured photoluminescence excitation (PLE) and photoluminescence emission (PL) for all three samples. The results are shown in Fig. 6(a–f).

The PLE spectra for sample HCD1 (synthesized from L-ascorbic acid precursor) show a maximum at 353 nm when monitoring emission at 430 nm. However, when the emission wavelength is fixed at 386 and 520 nm, the PLE spectra exhibit two separate bands at 310 and 363 nm, suggesting that the prominent band at 353 nm is a culmination of these two separate excitation bands. This is illustrated in Fig. 6(a). The fitting of the PLE spectrum of sample HCD1 is also shown in supplementary Figure S1. When fixing the excitation wavelengths at 310, 353, and 363 nm, the PL emission maxima are obtained at 386, 430, and 520 nm, respectively. The normalized PL emission spectra for this sample are shown in Fig. 6(b). A shift of approximately 15 nm (from 415 to 430 nm) is observed for excitation at different wavelengths, along with a change in the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the various samples. This blue emission is often attributed to specific surface states and functional groups present on the CDs, such as COOH, -OH, and NH
2
, which contribute to fluorescence. Surface defects, along with oxygen-containing groups, can also contribute to this blue emission.

For sample HCD2 (synthesized using a sodium carbonate precursor), the PLE and PL emission spectra are shown in Fig. 6(c). Two distinct PLE bands are observed at 305 nm and 305 + 365 nm when monitoring PL emission at the maxima of 420 nm and 460 nm, respectively. The position of the 305 nm band remains consistent in both cases; however, an additional band at 365 nm appears when the emission is fixed at 460 nm. The prominent PL emissions occur at 420 nm and 460 nm, with a shift of ∼ 40 nm between the two emission spectra, suggesting that the origins and charge relaxation pathways of the two spectra are completely different. The normalized PL emission spectra are also shown in Fig. 6(d) for a comparison and better visibility. The fitting of the PLE spectrum of sample HCD2 is also shown in supplementary Figure S2.

For sample HCD3 (prepared from urea), three prominent PLE bands are obtained at 280, 310 and 355 nm. The results are shown in Fig. 6(e). Upon excitation with these bands, the PL emission spectra shows emission at different wavelengths, for example, upon excitation at 280 nm, the maximum is obtained at 330 and 430 nm. However, upon excitation at 310 and 355 nm, the PL emission maxima are obtained at 410 and 440 nm, respectively. Some additional kinks are also observed at 345 and 405 nm,
c f
. Fig. 6(f). The fitting of the PLE spectrum of sample HCD3 is also shown in supplementary Figure S3.

The PL properties of carbon dots (whether graphitic or carbon-based) depend on the electronic states and their population in the π domains of the particle core, which are sp
2
hybridized and conjugated. In the literature, the band gap of carbon dots is calculated using density functional theory, and it has been observed that it can vary between 7 and 2 eV (or 235 and 1000 nm) for particles with sizes ranging from 0.46 to 2.31 nm [35,36]. This variation can be attributed to the quantum confinement effect. The carbon dots primarily consist of sp
3
hybridized carbon atoms along with various functional groups such as C-O-C, C-O, C=O, and COOH. The presence of these functionalities leads to the formation of several emissive state traps (or surface states), and the PL in this case can result from a combination of one or several of these components formed during the synthesis process.

Considering our PLE and PL emission spectra for different samples, we observe that the prominent emission in each case is in blue-region of the electromagnetic spectrum, while the PLE is different than the UV-visible absorption spectra [Fig. 5]. This suggests that the surface states (defects) dominate in our samples and are responsible for luminescence of blue color. PLE has prominent two bands: one in the deep UV (around 280/310 nm), while the other one around 360 ± 5 nm. The latter PLE band which is common in all the samples overlap closely with the near-UV converted solid-state lighting devices. We calculated CIE coordinates from the PL emission spectra of all the samples for their suitability and color purity in lighting devices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photostability, color purity and lighting application
ce:section.ce:para: The photostability of carbon dots is a critical factor in evaluating their potential for use in optical devices, as it directly influences their durability and performance under continuous light exposure. To assess this parameter, we exposed our carbon dot samples to a 40 W lamp at room temperature for varying durations, with exposure times ranging from 5 to 60 min. This method simulates the conditions that the materials may experience in practical optical applications, allowing us to investigate how their optical properties evolve under prolonged light exposure. The results of this experiment, shown in Fig. 7, provide valuable insights into the stability of the carbon dots’ optical characteristics. By comparing the luminescence intensity before and after different exposure times, we can determine the extent of photobleaching or any other degradation in performance. The findings highlight the time-dependent changes in the photophysical properties of the carbon dots, which are essential for their optimization in long-term optical device applications.

The samples HCD1 and HCD2 demonstrate superior photostability and time-stability when compared to the HCD3 sample, as evidenced by their relatively consistent optical performance even after prolonged exposure to external light. These samples maintain a high level of luminescence intensity with minimal degradation, showing only a slight reduction in intensity, which is within a margin of ± 1%. This low level of variation indicates that HCD1 and HCD2 possess excellent resistance to photobleaching and other forms of light-induced degradation. In contrast, the HCD3 sample exhibits comparably more significant reduction in luminescence intensity over time ( ∼ 5%), suggesting lower photostability under the same exposure conditions. Despite these differences, the overall stability of HCD1, HCD2 and HCD3 samples makes them promising candidates for practical applications in lighting devices, where prolonged exposure to light is a common operational condition.

The minimal change in optical properties for different samples, even after extended light exposure, confirms their potential for use in long-term, high-performance lighting applications. The observed stability could lead to longer-lasting, more efficient lighting solutions, where the maintenance of color purity and intensity is critical for the device’s performance and lifespan.

The color purity of the three samples is determined by evaluating chromaticity commission International de l’Eclairage (CIE1931) coordinates. The values are shown in CIE diagram in Fig. 8(a). The two independent variables x and y are presented rather than mathematically possible three coordinates x, y, z. Herein, x + y + z = 1, and the value depends upon three tristimulus values X, Y, and Z. The values for sample HCD1 is evaluated to be (0.1550, 0.1443), while the values for HCD2 and HCD3 are evaluated to be (0.1532, 0.2352) and (0.1539, 0.1487),
c f
. Fig. 8(b).

When the values for three samples are compared with the standard value of blue color (0.1400, 0.1000) [37], the PL emission spectrum of HCD3 sample appears close to pure blue color. The actual color purity(in %) is determine using Eq. (6).
(6)
P u r i t y
( % )
= 100 ×
( x −
x
r e f .
)
2
+
( y −
y
r e f .
)
2
(
x
b l u e
−
x
r e f .
)
2
+
(
y
b l u e
−
y
r e f .
)
2
where, (x, y), (x
r e f
,
y
r e f
), (
x
b l u e
,
y
b l u e
) are coordinates of HCD1/HCD2/ HCD3 sample, reference illuminant for CIE1931 (0.3333, 0.3333), and of the blue color (0.1400, 0.1000), respectively. The purity percentages for the three samples are calculated to be 85.7% for HCD1, 67.7% for HCD2, and 84.9% for HCD3. These purity values suggest that HCD1 and HCD3 are high-color-purity samples and therefore have significant potential for use in solid-state lighting devices. In HCD2, sodium carbonate may introduce the trap states leading to peak broadening and shift in PL emission maximum to higher wavelength. Another important parameter, the thermal quenching temperature, could not be determined during this study due to experimental limitations, highlighting the pathway for further research.

We presented a simple synthesis of three carbon dot samples using the hydrothermal synthesis method. The precursors used were L-ascorbic acid, sodium carbonate, and urea. The XRD spectra of all the samples exhibit strong (hkl) reflections of carbon dots, confirming their formation. The W–H plot shows that there is strain in the lattice, and upon considering contributions from the same, the diameter of the crystallites was found to be ∼ 65 nm. The results were complemented by data from a zeta sizer. The surface charge (potential) of all the samples is found to be negative, suggesting the possibility for using this sample set in biological applications. The formation of carbon dots was further confirmed by FTIR studies. The UV-visible reflectance spectra suggest that the samples possess two different signatures: one in the UV region due to the graphitic core, and the second in the near-UV and visible region due to surface defects. The PL excitation and emission spectra are dominated by surface defects, which impact the position of the PL emission spectra in all three samples. Upon using the PL emission spectra for near-UV converted solid-state lighting devices, we observe that the chromaticity coordinates closely match the standard blue emission spectra. The color purity of the HCD1 and HCD3 samples is determined to be approximately 85%, suggesting their potential use in lighting applications.

Priti Manon: Writing – original draft, Software, Formal analysis, Data curation. Anil Arya: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Suchinder K. Sharma: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Suchinder Sharma reports financial support was provided by Science and Engineering Research Board. Suchinder Sharma reports a relationship with Science and Engineering Research Board that includes: funding grants. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Suchinder K. Sharma thanks DST-SERB India (SRG/2022/000415) for their financial support. This work was partially carried out using the facilities of Central Instrumentation Facility (CIF), Amity University Punjab, Mohali, India .

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2024.113745.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary material related to this article.
MMC S1
Figure S1, S2 and S3 present fitting of PL emission spectra into different components.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Ms. Priti Manon is currently pursuing a Ph.D. under the main supervision of Dr. Suchinder K. Sharma at the Amity School of Physical Sciences. She completed her M.Sc. Physics in 2015 with 3rd rank in the university. Her research is centered on the synthesis of carbon dots and the exploration of their structural, optical, and sensing properties. She is member of Indian Association of Physics Teachers’ (IAPT) in India.

Dr. Anil Arya is currently an Assistant Professor in the Department of Physics at Amity University, Punjab, Mohali, India. He obtained his master’s and Ph.D. degrees from the Department of Physics, Central University of Punjab, Bathinda, in 2015 and 2019, respectively. He qualified for NET (CSIR) in 2014 and GATE in 2015. Dr. Arya worked as a D.S. Kothari Postdoctoral Fellow in the Department of Physics at Kurukshetra University, Kurukshetra, for two years. He has authored and co-authored over 80 publications in peer-reviewed journals, including research articles, review articles, conference proceedings, and more than 15 book chapters with international publishers. He has four years of research experience. His research interests include the synthesis of nanostructured materials, polymer electrolytes, and activated carbon derived from bio-waste, as well as their applications in energy storage and conversion devices.

Dr. Suchinder Sharma has been working as Associate Professor and Head of the School of Physical Sciences, Amity University Punjab, since June 2021. He completed his Ph.D. from NIT Bhopal, followed by postdoctoral positions at PRL Ahmedabad (India) from 2010–11 and Chimie Paristech, Paris (France) from 2011–2014. Dr. Sharma served as a Visiting Scientist at PRL Ahmedabad, then as a Scientist at Chalmers University of Technology, Gothenburg (2015–2017), and at TU Bergakademie Freiberg, Germany (2017–2021). He is currently leading two funded projects from SERB and UGC-DAE consortium. His research focuses on UV and near-infrared persistent luminescent materials for phototherapy and bioimaging, as well as carbon dots for optical devices.
